title: The Banker’s Secret
blurb: A Medici banker by day. Puppet master by night. Every loan signed. Every rival destroyed. Until the ledgers burn the city to the ground.
comparable: For fans of *The Merchant of Venice* meets *The Name of the Rose* in a duel of ledgers and daggers.

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

The Mercato Nuovo’s marble floor gleamed under the morning sun, polished daily to show off Florence’s wealth to the world—or so the merchants claimed. Lorenzo di Filippo Strozzi adjusted his cuffs, the dark wool too heavy for May but necessary to hide the ink stains beneath. The scribe before him, a fresh-faced boy no older than sixteen, trembled as he held out the ledger.

“Here, my lord. The exchange rate for the Bruges shipment.”

Lorenzo didn’t look up. His fingers traced the margins of the page, lingering over smudged numerals. “Bruges charged us for transport twice. The ledger says once.” The boy’s Adam’s apple bobbed. Lorenzo exhaled through his nose. “Count it again. Slowly.”

The Medici clerks standing nearby exchanged glances. One smirked. The other flicked a coin across his knuckles. They thought this was patience. They thought this was discipline.

A cough cut through the murmurs of the money changers. A friar in a rough-spun robe stood just beyond the colonnade, his gaze fixed on the street’s far end. Girolamo Savonarola. Lorenzo had seen him before in the Piazza della Signoria, always watching, always waiting for Florence to rot from within. The friar’s presence coiled in the air like a held breath.

The scribe’s quill scratched. A bead of sweat rolled down his temple. “It was a mistake, my lord. I’ll correct it at once.”

Lorenzo’s pen hovered over the ledger. “No. Leave it. A man who cannot learn from his errors is soon ruined by them.” The boy paled. Good. Fear made clerks precise.

Agnolo Poliziano appeared at Lorenzo’s elbow, his velvet doublet brushing against wool. “Lorenzo. The Pazzi consignment is late. Contessina’s agent says they’ll need the funds by week’s end or she walks.”

Lorenzo didn’t look up. “Then she walks. The Pazzi name carries no weight now.” His pen moved, correcting the scribe’s ledger without touching it, sliding the ink to where it should be. A whisper of power, nothing more.

Poliziano’s fingers tapped his thigh. “You’re playing with fire. The Pazzi won’t forget.”

“Nor will I.” Lorenzo’s voice was soft, a banker’s murmur. He turned the page. There, half-hidden beneath the Medici seal, the same hand had written three letters in the margin: L.d.P.S. His initials. His signature. His thumb smudged the wet ink.

The scribe’s eyes darted to the letters, then away. “Shall I—?”

“No.” The word snapped like a whip. The boy flinched. Lorenzo’s smile was a blade wrapped in velvet. “It’s perfectly clear.”

A ripple of laughter ran through the Medici clerks. One bowed. “As you say, my lord.”

Poliziano’s voice dropped. “You’re taunting them. The Pazzi aren’t the only ones who remember what the Medici did to their fathers.”

Lorenzo closed the ledger. “I’m not taunting anyone.” He handed the book to a clerk. “See it goes to the vault.” The Medici clerks closed ranks around it like guard dogs.

The friar—still watching—turned and melted into the crowd.

Poliziano exhaled sharply through his nose. “You’re slipping. Savonarola’s here for a reason.”

Lorenzo adjusted his signet ring, a Medici lion winking in the light. “Let him watch. Florence burns slow.” He strode toward the exit, his boots clicking on marble. Poliziano followed, a shadow that couldn’t be shaken.

Beyond the colonnade, the Mercato buzzed with merchants haggling in three languages, their voices a sea of greed and hunger. Lorenzo paused at the edge, his shadow stretching long across the cobblestones. In the Mercato Nuovo, every deal was a debt waiting to be called in. Every ledger a weapon.

He didn’t see Contessina Pazzi watching from a balcony above the goldsmith’s stall, her gloved hand resting on the stone railing, her dark eyes fixed on him with something like recognition—or hate. But he felt her gaze like a blade between his ribs.

Poliziano noticed. “She’s been here every day this week.”

Lorenzo didn’t turn. “Let her watch.”

The crowd shifted. A cart laden with bolts of Florentine cloth rolled past, blocking the view for a heartbeat. When it cleared, Contessina Pazzi was gone.

Poliziano’s fingers tightened on his scroll. “You’re playing a dangerous game, Lorenzo. With her. With the city. With yourself.”

Lorenzo adjusted his gloves, one finger at a time. “I play to win.”

He walked on, the Medici lion stamped on his ring glinting like a promise—or a threat. Behind him, the ledger’s margin whispered its secret to the stone floor, just one more unreadable thing in a city of lies.

Chapter 2

The Piazza della Signoria pulsed with torchlight and the scent of roasted capon. Half of Florence had turned out for San Giovanni, their faces flushed from wine and summer heat. Lorenzo adjusted the stiff linen at his throat, the Medici colors too loud for his taste, but silence was a tighter collar still.

Across the square, Lorenzo de’ Medici stood beneath the Loggia dei Lanzi, his crimson doublet catching the firelight like fresh blood. The Magnificent raised a goblet. The crowd hushed.

“Tonight we honor the hand that feeds Florence,” he called, voice carrying like a priest’s blessing. “Even those who once fed at our table.”

Lorenzo’s fingers twitched at his side. He recognized the script. The words were a blade wrapped in silk.

“Our dear friends the Strozzi,” Lorenzo de’ Medici continued, “once housed the most loyal of Medici moneylenders in their vaults. When the Strozzi fled, we kept their bank alive. We kept their people fed. We kept their name upon our lips.”

Poliziano’s knuckles turned white around his goblet. He didn’t sip. He didn’t breathe.

The Magnificent turned toward the shadows near the fountain. “Gentlemen, meet Lorenzo di Filippo Strozzi. The last of his line. A banker by our grace.”

Laughter rippled through the nobles. A few faces Lorenzo recognized—men who had signed his father’s ruin with trembling hands.

Contessina Pazzi stood at the edge of the press of bodies, her black dress a stain against marble. Her gaze locked onto his—not with recognition, but with something colder. Not hatred. Not yet. Something older. Resignation.

Lorenzo de’ Medici gestured to a young man in Medici livery. “Bring forth the ledger.”

A clerk emerged from the crowd, bowing so low his spine creased. He held out a massive volume, its cover stamped with the Strozzi crest—faded now, scarred by time. The clerk’s hands trembled as he presented it to the Magnificent.

“Behold,” Lorenzo de’ Medici said, flipping the pages with deliberate slowness. “The records of the Strozzi Bank, preserved these twenty years in our vaults. Every loan. Every receipt. Every penny owed to the Strozzi.” His voice dropped. “Or so they thought.”

He snapped the book shut. “In truth, my friends, the Strozzi Bank was a Medici front from its first coin. Funded by our charity. Administered by our men. And when it failed—they failed.” He tossed the ledger into the fountain. It landed with a heavy splash, pages splaying like broken wings.

The crowd gasped. Some laughed.

Poliziano’s goblet shattered in his grip. Wine dripped between his fingers, dark as old bruises.

Contessina Pazzi didn’t move. Didn’t blink. Her lips parted, just slightly, as if she meant to speak—but no sound came. Then she turned and vanished into the press.

Lorenzo felt the silence settle over him like a shroud. He was no longer a banker in a Medici world. He was a ghost summoned only to be buried again.

“A toast,” Lorenzo de’ Medici announced, raising his goblet once more. “To Florence. And to the mercy we show even our enemies.”

The crowd echoed the toast. Voices thick with wine, with hunger, with the need to belong.

Lorenzo walked toward the Palazzo Medici, his boots heavy on the cobblestones. Poliziano fell into step beside him, his face a mask of controlled fury.

“You let him do that,” Poliziano said quietly, not looking at him. “You let him humiliate you in front of the city.”

Lorenzo didn’t answer. He didn’t need to. They both knew the truth: every word tonight had been scripted. Every humiliation was a rehearsal.

Sandro Botticelli emerged from the crowd, his sketchbook tucked under one arm. His eyes gleamed with the thrill of witness. “My lord Strozzi,” he said, bowing with exaggerated flourish. “What a profile! The bone beneath the skin—so rarely seen in our circles.” He flipped open the book and showed Lorenzo the page: a quick charcoal sketch of his face, sharp as a blade.

Lorenzo’s gaze flicked to it. A single detail stood out: a jagged line across his left cheekbone, a scribe’s error magnified into a scar.

His hand went to his face, unconsciously. The scar wasn’t there. Never had been.

Botticelli smiled, as if he’d discovered a secret worth keeping. “Art catches what the eye misses. Or reveals what the hand conceals.”

Poliziano’s jaw tightened. “Botticelli, enough.”

The artist bowed again, mockingly this time. “I only honor the moment.” He snapped the sketchbook shut. “Tomorrow, the page will be in every atelier in Florence. The world will see the face of a banker who isn’t what he seems.”

Lorenzo felt the weight of the lie settle on his shoulders. He had been too careful. Too hidden. And now, in one night, the Medici had unmasked him not as a failure, but as something worse: a fraud.

Botticelli drifted away, swallowed by the revelry.

Poliziano exhaled sharply. “You’re bleeding.”

Lorenzo looked down. A trickle of blood ran from his palm—he’d unknowingly clenched his signet ring too tightly. He wiped it on his sleeve.

“They’ll call you the Man Who Wasn’t There,” Poliziano said. “The banker who never was.”

Lorenzo turned toward the Palazzo Medici, its windows glowing like embers in the dark. “Then let them call me what I am.”

He walked faster, leaving Poliziano behind. The crowd parted for him, but their eyes followed. Whispers coiled after him like smoke.

He reached the palace gates. A guard stood stiffly at attention. “My lord Strozzi.”

Lorenzo paused. “Yes?”

“His Magnificence requests your presence at dawn. In the old vault.”

Lorenzo felt the ground tilt beneath him. The Medici vault. The one no outsider entered. The one that held the real ledgers—the ones that proved every noble in Florence was a debtor to a ghost.

He stepped through the gate, the iron clanging shut behind him.

In the distance, beyond the city walls, the Arno river stretched black under the moon. Florence slept. But sleep was only the calm before the storm.

He didn’t see Contessina Pazzi watching from the shadows of the Loggia, her dark eyes burning with a fire of her own.

But he felt her gaze, like a brand pressed to his spine.

Chapter 3

The Church of San Marco smelled of damp stone and old wax. Lorenzo knelt before the confessional, his fingers tracing the grooves of the wood—once smooth, now pitted with centuries of whispered sins. He pressed the tip of his knife into the grain, slow, deliberate. The blade bit deep enough to draw a thin line of blood from his palm, but he didn’t flinch.

Outside, the city’s murmurs seeped through the cracks in the door. Poliziano’s voice rose, sharp with frustration. “You cannot silence a city by burning its books, Fra Girolamo.”

A friar’s dry chuckle. “And you cannot feed a city on empty promises, Agnolo. The people are hungry for truth, not pennies.”

Lorenzo didn’t listen. He carved deeper, the knife following the path of the ledger’s coded inscription. The letters were burned into his memory: *F, L, 4.3.1478*. A signature of ruin. A promise kept.

The knife slipped.

The blade caught the wood, splintering under the pressure. The date *1 April 1478* split the confessional door like a wound. Lorenzo froze. The numbers glared up at him, black and final.

A shadow shifted in the aisle.

Lorenzo turned his head slightly. Fra Savonarola stood ten paces away, his back to the altar, his eyes half-lidded in prayer. Or perhaps in watchfulness.

“You’re late with your confession,” the friar said.

Lorenzo withdrew the knife. “Not everything is a sin to confess.”

“Everything is a confession in the eyes of God.”

A floorboard creaked behind the confessional. Poliziano’s footsteps, pacing. The friar’s gaze flicked toward the sound, then back to Lorenzo.

“You don’t pray,” Savonarola observed. “You brood.”

Lorenzo wiped the blade on his sleeve. “I prefer action to penance.”

“And yet here you are, carving your regrets into sacred wood.”

“Regrets are for those who believe in mercy.”

Savonarola exhaled through his nose. “The Magnificent believes in strength. That is his religion.” He stepped closer. “You are a banker, Lorenzo. You deal in numbers, not absolution.”

“Numbers tell the truth,” Lorenzo said. “Mercy is just another ledger entry.”

Poliziano’s voice cut through the quiet. “Fra Girolamo, the people are gathering outside. They’re demanding bread and answers.”

The friar didn’t look away from Lorenzo. “Then give them bread. Or give them chaos. But do not pretend the choice is yours alone.”

Lorenzo slid the knife home. “I don’t make choices. I correct them.”

Savonarola’s lips twitched. A smile, or a warning. “Then correct carefully. Florence is not a ledger. It bleeds when you tear a page.”

Poliziano appeared at the end of the aisle, his face tight. “Lorenzo. We have to go.”

Lorenzo rose. The carved date stared up at him. He stepped over it without looking back.

As they walked toward the heavy oak doors, Poliziano lowered his voice. “You came here to carve, not to pray. I know the difference.”

Lorenzo didn’t answer. The sunlight outside was too bright after the dim church. Poliziano squinted, scanning the square. “Savonarola’s right about one thing. The people are restless. And the Magnificent is making it worse.”

Lorenzo’s hand went to his face. The skin under his fingers felt rough where the knife had slipped. No cut. Just the phantom burn of the ledger’s inscription.

“He wants my ledgers,” Lorenzo said. “All of them.”

“You don’t have ledgers. You have a shadow.”

“Then he’ll find the shadow and mistake it for a man.”

Poliziano grabbed Lorenzo’s arm as he stepped into the square. “You’re playing with fire. The Pazzi anniversary is coming. The city’s on edge.”

Lorenzo looked across the Mercato Nuovo. Merchants were striking tents, packing scales and balances. A banker in Medici blue moved between them, counting coins with a smirk.

“He’s gloating,” Poliziano muttered. “He thinks he’s won.”

Lorenzo watched the banker slip a ledger under his arm. The cover bore the Medici crest, fresh gilt gleaming in the sun.

“He hasn’t won anything,” Lorenzo said. “He’s just forgotten he ever owed a debt.”

Poliziano’s eyes narrowed. “What did you just—?”

Lorenzo walked away. Poliziano caught up, his voice low and urgent. “You’re not thinking of giving him what he wants.”

“He doesn’t know what he wants.”

“Then enlighten me.”

“He wants control. He wants order. He wants the ledgers of a bank that doesn’t exist.” Lorenzo turned into a narrow alley, Poliziano close behind. “But the real ledgers—the ones that prove every noble bowed to the Medici—they’re already gone.”

Poliziano grabbed his shoulder. “Where?”

Lorenzo brushed off the grip. “Somewhere safe.”

“From whom?”

“From him.”

Poliziano’s breath hitched. “You’ve been building a counter-ledge. All this time—”

“Not a ledger. A weapon.”

The alley opened into the Piazza San Marco. A crowd had gathered, half in shade, half in sun. Men argued in low voices. Women clutched children close. A boy no older than twelve held a wooden sword, its tip wrapped in bloodied cloth.

Lorenzo stopped.

Contessina Pazzi stood at the edge of the crowd, her black dress stark against the sunlight. She wasn’t looking at the boy, or the argument, or the friar who had begun to preach from the steps of the church. She was looking at Lorenzo.

Her gaze didn’t flicker. Didn’t soften. It burned through him like a through a fresco, revealing the cracks beneath the plaster.

Poliziano followed his stare. “She’s been watching you since San Giovanni.”

Lorenzo didn’t answer.

Contessina took a step forward. Then another. The crowd parted for her without seeming to notice.

She stopped just beyond arm’s reach. “You carved a date into the confessional door.”

Lorenzo didn’t blink. “You were there?”

“Not in body. But in spirit.” She tilted her head slightly. “You and I share a debt to the Medici. You repay yours with ink and knives. I repay mine with silence and fire.”

Lorenzo felt Poliziano stiffen beside him. The poet’s voice was a whisper. “Contessina—”

She didn’t look at him. “Poliziano. Always in the middle.”

Poliziano’s jaw tightened. “Not by choice.”

Contessina’s gaze returned to Lorenzo. “The Pazzi anniversary is five days away. The Magnificent will be celebrating in the Palazzo. The city will be watching.”

“And?”

“And Savonarola will preach tomorrow. He’ll call for change. For truth.” She stepped closer. “The ledgers you carved into the wood—do they prove the Medici stole from Florence?”

Lorenzo didn’t answer.

She smiled, thin and sharp. “I didn’t think so. But you know where the real ones are.”

Poliziano’s voice cut in, high and tight. “Contessina, enough.”

She ignored him. “I’ll be at the Mercato Nuovo at the third hour tomorrow. Bring what you’ve taken. Or don’t.” She turned. “I’ll still burn his ledgers for him.”

She walked away, her dress whispering against the cobblestones. The crowd closed behind her like water.

Poliziano exhaled sharply. “She’s going to get you killed.”

Lorenzo watched her vanish into the press of bodies. “Or I’ll get her there first.”

Poliziano grabbed his arm. “This isn’t revenge. It’s suicide. And you’re dragging her into it.”

Lorenzo pulled free. “She’s not a pawn. She’s a firebrand. And fires need tending.”

Poliziano’s eyes darkened. “You’re playing with forces you don’t understand.”

Lorenzo looked back toward the church. The confessional door stood slightly ajar. The carved date stared back at him, dark and unyielding.

“Then let me understand them,” he said. “Before it’s too late.”

Chapter 4

The Palazzo Medici smelled of candle wax and silk. Lorenzo de’ Medici stood in the center of the private council chamber, his velvet doublet the deep red of ripe pomegranate. Sunlight slanted through the arched windows, gilding the ledgers spread across the table like a blessing.

Five men in Medici blue leaned over the documents, their fingers tracing columns of numbers. Poliziano hovered near the brazier, his poet’s hands folded tight at his waist.

“Here,” Lorenzo said, tapping a page. “The Bruges outpost. A simple correction to the exchange rates.”

One of the bankers, Messer Capponi, squinted. “That shifts half the profit to Rome.”

“Rome is where the debt is owed.” Lorenzo smiled. “Efficiency, Messer. The art of making money work harder than men.”

Capponi didn’t smile back. He adjusted his spectacles, studied the figures again. “This ledger hasn’t been updated in three months.”

“Because we’ve been busy.” Lorenzo picked up a second volume. “And here—Venice. The silk trade. A modest adjustment to the duty, but when scaled across every bolt—”

Poliziano cleared his throat. “Lorenzo.”

He didn’t look up. “Yes, Agnolo?”

“This ledger is supposed to be a record of assets. Not a ledger of debts in disguise.”

Lorenzo turned a page. “Assets are what we claim. Debts are what we control.”

A silence settled over the room. The bankers shifted, exchanging glances. Capponi’s jaw tightened.

Poliziano stepped forward. “The balance is off by nearly twenty percent.”

Lorenzo closed the volume with a snap. “Balances are for tax collectors. We deal in leverage.”

Messer Capponi’s pen snapped in his fingers. He didn’t seem to notice. “Messer Strozzi, if this is some jest—”

“Jests don’t buy palaces in Rome.” Lorenzo tossed the broken pen onto the table. “Gentlemen, the Medici Bank is not merely solvent. It is *liquid*. And liquidity is power.”

One of the younger bankers, a Medici cousin named Ridolfi, leaned toward Capponi. “He’s right. The King of Naples wants a loan. Double the usual sum. This ledger would justify it.”

Capponi stared at Lorenzo. “You’re proposing we lend to Ferrante?”

“Why not?” Lorenzo picked up a ledger, flipping pages. “The King’s treasury is stable. His exports of grain and alum are projected to rise. And with the right terms—”

“Terms that benefit the Medici first,” Poliziano muttered.

Ridolfi ignored him. “The King has been late on payments before.”

“Then we charge interest for the delay.” Lorenzo tapped the numbers. “See? Already accounted for.”

Capponi’s voice dropped. “This ledger is a fabrication.”

Lorenzo met his gaze. “It’s a ledger. And it says we can lend. That’s all that matters.”

The room grew still. The fire hissed. Poliziano’s fingers curled into fists.

Lorenzo de’ Medici clapped his hands once. “Gentlemen! The meeting is concluded. We act by week’s end.”

The bankers gathered their ledgers in silence. Capponi lingered at the door.

“Messer Strozzi,” he said quietly. “I’ve known the Medici for thirty years. I’ve never seen a ledger so… forgiving.”

Lorenzo didn’t look up from his desk. “Then you haven’t been paying attention.”

The door closed. Poliziano exhaled through his nose.

“You’ve cracked the ledger,” he said. “Not the Bank.”

Lorenzo didn’t answer. He shuffled papers, lining up the edges with surgical precision.

Poliziano moved closer. “You’re building a noose. You just handed him the rope.”

Lorenzo finally looked at him. “He thinks he’s holding the noose. That’s the beauty of it.”

Poliziano’s jaw clenched. “He’ll see the deception when the Naples loan defaults.”

“By then,” Lorenzo said, “he’ll have spent the money. And Florence will be too busy buying bread to notice the ledger errors.”

Poliziano stepped back. “You’re not just manipulating ledgers. You’re manipulating time.”

Lorenzo smiled. “Time is the only currency that never devalues.”

Poliziano turned toward the corridor. “I need the privy.”

Lorenzo didn’t look up. “Third door on the right.”

Poliziano left. The moment the latch clicked, Lorenzo’s smile faded. He moved to the door, slipped a hand into the gap, and felt the parchment before he saw it.

It had been slipped under, edges frayed from tight rolling. One glance told him everything.

A list of debts. Owed to the *Strozzi Bank*.

Not the Medici.

His.

He smoothed the sheet against his thigh. The ink was still wet. Fresh. Delivered in the last minute, by someone who knew.

He crumpled the parchment in his fist.

Poliziano returned, washing his hands in the basin by the door. “Still here?”

Lorenzo dropped the scrap of paper into the fire.

“Yes,” he said. “Still here.”

Chapter 5

The courtyard of the Medici palace was a garden of knives—ornamental, sharp, and eager to draw blood. Lorenzo de’ Medici moved between the boxwood hedges like a man inspecting livestock, his sleeves rolled to the elbow despite the autumn chill. Poliziano followed at a respectful distance, his poet’s cloak dragging through the gravel.

“You sent the Naples ledger,” Poliziano said.

Lorenzo plucked a fig from a low branch. The skin split under his thumbnail. “I did.”

“Capponi thinks you’re cooking the books.”

“Capponi thinks in columns. I think in leverage.” Lorenzo flicked the fruit into a waiting servant’s basket. “He’s no danger to us.”

“Men who think in columns run banks.”

“And men who run banks die of boredom.” Lorenzo wiped his thumb on his doublet. “Send the Naples contract to Ferrante by messenger. Tonight. Before anyone has time to count the zeros twice.”

Poliziano stepped into the path, forcing Lorenzo to stop. “You’re lending to a king who can’t pay his own guards. That’s not leverage. That’s a gift.”

“Gifts cement loyalty.” Lorenzo sidestepped, continuing toward the shaded colonnade. “And loyalty is what keeps Florence from burning again.”

“You sound like him.”

The words landed like a thrown stone. Poliziano never invoked the name. Not in public. Not ever.

Lorenzo turned. “Like who?”

“Like the man who taught you how to fold a ledger.” Poliziano’s voice dropped. “Like the man who ruined your father.”

The colonnade’s arch cast a long shadow over Lorenzo’s face. He didn’t flinch. “You mistake gratitude for mimicry.”

“Do I?” Poliziano’s eyes flicked to the palace windows. “He’s in there now. Watching. Waiting.”

Lorenzo walked on. “He’s always watching. It’s why we’re still standing.”

Poliziano caught his arm. The grip was light, but firm. “You’re not standing. You’re drowning. And you’re taking the Bank with you.”

Lorenzo pulled free. “The Bank is a boat. I’m the current.”

A servant approached, bowing low. “Messer Strozzi. A letter from Bruges.”

Lorenzo took the rolled parchment, broke the wax seal. The ink was still damp. The hand was a Medici cipher, familiar and hated.

He unrolled it on the stone balustrade.

*To the Magnificent,*

*The Bruges ledger discrepancy persists. The exchange rate adjustment you proposed does not match our records. The Strozzi ledger shows—*

The rest was torn away, the edge jagged as a knife slash.

Lorenzo crumpled the parchment. His knuckles whitened.

Poliziano leaned in. “What does it say?”

Lorenzo didn’t answer. He strode through the courtyard, boots clicking against the cobblestones, ignoring the puzzled servants, the rustling fig leaves, the world that suddenly felt too small.

Poliziano kept pace. “Lorenzo.”

“It says we’re being watched.” Lorenzo didn’t look back. “By the ghosts we hired.”

They reached the private study. Lorenzo shoved the door open. Inside, the air smelled of leather and old wine. A fire crackled in the hearth. Lorenzo went straight to the desk, pulled a ledger from the stack, flipped pages with sharp, precise movements.

Poliziano closed the door. “You knew this would happen.”

“Of course I knew.” Lorenzo’s voice was low, controlled. “I planned for it.”

“Then why does your hand shake?”

Lorenzo’s fingers stilled. He didn’t look up. “It doesn’t.”

Poliziano crossed the room, snatched the ledger from Lorenzo’s hands. He scanned the pages, his lips moving silently. Then he stopped. His breath caught.

“Twenty percent.”

Lorenzo didn’t deny it.

Poliziano looked up. “You stole twenty percent from the Medici. All of it. Every ledger. Every branch. You’ve been siphoning funds for years.”

“Yes.”

“And you called it a correction.”

“Language is a ledger too.” Lorenzo took the book back, smoothed the pages. “Every word carries weight. Every ledger carries debt. The trick is to make the debt someone else’s.”

Poliziano’s face darkened. “You’re not building a fortune. You’re building a confession.”

Lorenzo set the ledger down. “Confessions are for priests. I deal in contracts.”

A knock at the door. Before either could answer, it swung open. Contessina Pazzi stood in the threshold, her dark dress stark against the wooden doorframe. Her gaze locked onto Lorenzo’s face, then flicked to Poliziano, then back.

No one spoke.

Contessina stepped inside. “I heard you’re lending the King of Naples twenty thousand florins.”

Lorenzo inclined his head. “Twenty-five. With favorable terms.”

“Terms that benefit the Medici first,” she said.

Poliziano exhaled through his nose. “Always a pleasure, Contessina.”

She ignored him. “You’re hemorrhaging gold for a man who can’t repay.”

Lorenzo leaned back against the desk. “Gold isn’t gold until it’s spent in the right place.”

“Spent on what? Another palace? Another bribe?”

“On stability.”

“Stability?” Contessina’s laugh was sharp. “You mean the kind that buries families in unmarked graves.”

A silence settled, thick as smoke. Poliziano shifted, uncomfortable. Lorenzo didn’t move.

Contessina stepped closer. “You think you’re playing a game. But you’re not the only one with ledgers.”

Lorenzo’s eyes narrowed. “What do you mean?”

“My family kept records too.” She reached into her sleeve, produced a folded parchment. “Records of what the Medici took. From us. From the city. From the poor.”

She tossed the paper onto the desk. It unfurled, revealing neat columns of figures, dates, names.

Lorenzo didn’t touch it. “And?”

“And I know about the Strozzi ledger.”

Poliziano stiffened. Lorenzo didn’t blink.

Contessina continued, voice low. “I know you’re not Strozzi. I know you’re Medici in hiding. I know you’ve been bleeding your own family dry for twenty years.”

Lorenzo’s jaw tightened. “You know nothing.”

“I know you’re about to make the same mistake the Pazzi made.” She stepped back toward the door. “You’re trusting numbers over knives.”

She left without waiting for an answer.

The door closed. Poliziano exhaled. “She’s dangerous.”

Lorenzo picked up the Pazzi parchment, ripped it in half, then in half again. He dropped the pieces into the fire.

“She’s irrelevant,” he said.

Poliziano watched the flames consume the paper. “She’s not irrelevant. She’s a Pazzi. And the Pazzi remember.”

Lorenzo turned from the fire. “She’s a woman with a ledger and a grudge. That’s all she’ll ever be.”

Poliziano met his gaze. “You’re lying.”

Lorenzo walked to the window. The city of Florence stretched beyond the palace walls, rooftops gleaming in the late sun. He could see the Mercato Nuovo, the money changers’ stalls, the pulsing heart of the city’s wealth.

He spoke without turning around.

“Send the contract to Naples. Tonight.”

Poliziano stepped beside him. “And after that?”

Lorenzo watched a pigeon land on the palace roof, tilt its head as if listening.

“After that,” he said quietly, “we wait for the defaults to begin.”

Chapter 6

The Mercato Nuovo smelled of sweat and silver, the air thick with the clink of coins and the murmur of men who measured wealth in eyereach. Lorenzo stood beneath the portico of the Loggia del Pesce, his cloak pulled low, watching the money-changers at their tables. Their ledgers lay open, quills poised above parchment like surgeons over a patient ready to bleed.

He stepped forward, his boots clicking against the stone. A young scribe at the nearest table looked up, blinking in the afternoon light.

“Messer Strozzi,” the scribe said, bowing. “We weren’t expecting you.”

Lorenzo didn’t return the bow. “I’m here for the guilds’ ledgers.”

The scribe hesitated, then gestured to the stack of books beside him. “All present and accounted for, sir. The carpenters, the wool merchants, the tanners—”

“All recalled,” Lorenzo said.

The scribe’s face paled. “What?”

“Every outstanding Medici loan to the guilds. Due immediately.” Lorenzo’s voice was quiet, final. “No extensions. No negotiations.”

The scribe reached for a ledger, flipped it open. His fingers trembled as he ran his eyes down the columns of figures. “But… the terms aren’t—”

“Due immediately,” Lorenzo repeated. “By sundown.”

The scribe swallowed. “Messer Strozzi, the interest—”

“The interest is God’s concern.” Lorenzo turned to the next table, where a grizzled wool merchant sat counting coins into leather pouches. The merchant looked up, his face a map of surprise.

“Messer Strozzi,” the merchant said, pushing his ledger aside. “You can’t mean to—”

“I mean to close the account.” Lorenzo’s hand rested on the ledger. “Every florin the Medici has lent your guild. Every interest accrued. Today.”

The wool merchant’s eyes darted to his ledger, then back to Lorenzo. “The Medici will have something to say about this.”

“Let them.” Lorenzo’s fingers tapped the ledger’s cover. “I’ll wait.”

The merchant hesitated, then scribbled a note at the bottom of the page. He blew on the ink, pressed his seal into the wax. “Paid in full.”

Lorenzo didn’t acknowledge the note. He moved to the next table, where three guild masters huddled over a shared ledger. The carpenters. The bankers. The dyers. All flush with gold from Medici loans, all suddenly flush with confusion.

“Gentlemen,” Lorenzo said.

The carpenters’ master looked up, his beard bristling. “Messer Strozzi. What is this?”

“The end of your Medici debt.” Lorenzo tapped the ledger. “Consider it good fortune.”

The dyer’s master scoffed. “Good fortune? You call this good fortune?”

“Call it whatever you like.” Lorenzo’s voice was calm, unyielding. “The debt is closed.”

The bankers’ master leaned forward. “And the interest?”

“Paid in full.” Lorenzo’s gaze didn’t waver. “Every florin. With my compliments.”

The guild masters exchanged glances. The carpenters’ master flipped through the ledger, his brow furrowing. “But the interest rate here—it’s not possible. The balance doesn’t—”

“Does now.” Lorenzo closed the ledger with a sharp *thud*. “Take your coins. Spend them wisely.”

The guild masters stood in stunned silence as Lorenzo walked away, his cloak swaying behind him. He didn’t look back. Didn’t need to.

The money-changers watched him leave, their quills hovering above parchment, their ledgers suddenly irrelevant. The Mercato Nuovo buzzed with whispers, the clink of coins drowned out by murmurs of shock and confusion.

Lorenzo stepped into the shade of a nearby alley, his pulse steady. The gold wasn’t in the Medici vaults anymore. It was in the shadows, flowing back into the ledgers only he could see.

He exhaled, slow and deliberate, as if exhaling the last breath of a man who had just outlived his old life.

A hand clamped onto his shoulder.

Lorenzo turned. Poliziano stood there, his face unreadable, his cloak dusted with the grime of the marketplace.

“You’ve done it,” Poliziano said.

Lorenzo nodded. “Yes.”

“You’ve recalled every loan.”

“Yes.”

Poliziano’s grip tightened. “The guilds are in uproar. The treasurer is screaming for answers. The Medici are about to learn what happens when a banker pulls the plug.”

Lorenzo smirked. “Let them scream.”

Poliziano leaned in, his voice dropping to a hiss. “You’ve just made every guild master in Florence your enemy. And the Medici… they don’t forget.”

Lorenzo pulled free. “The Medici won’t remember their names by next week.”

“You’re playing a dangerous game.”

“No.” Lorenzo adjusted his cloak, hiding the sheen of sweat on his neck. “I’m playing the only game left.”

Poliziano’s eyes darkened. “And Contessina Pazzi?”

Lorenzo’s smirk faded. He didn’t answer.

Poliziano pressed. “She knows. She’s been watching you. Waiting.”

“Let her watch.” Lorenzo stepped past him, back into the sunlight. “Maybe she’ll learn something.”

Poliziano grabbed his arm again, forcing him to stop. “You’re not just recalling loans. You’re recalling the past. And the past always has teeth.”

Lorenzo met his gaze. “I know.”

Poliziano’s jaw clenched. “Then you know what happens when you pull the threads of a conspiracy this old.”

Lorenzo turned away. “I know exactly what happens.”

He walked back into the Mercato Nuovo, leaving Poliziano standing in the alley, his face pale with the weight of unspoken words.

The treasurer’s ledger lay open on a table nearby, its margins crowded with numbers, its columns filled with payments that hadn’t existed an hour ago.

Lorenzo paused beside it. His eyes flicked to the bottom right corner, where a single word had been scrawled in hurried ink:

*Paid.*

Chapter 7

The Mercato Nuovo seethed with midday heat and the copper tang of unsold fish. Lorenzo moved against the current, his borrowed cloak heavy with sweat, the hem frayed where some previous owner had dragged it through Florence’s filth. The money-changers’ tables had always been islands of order in this chaos—ledgers stacked like altars, quills poised, scales balanced for judgment. Today, two tables stood empty. No coins. No ledgers. Just nervous merchants shifting on their feet.

He stopped where the wool drapers usually sat. Empty. No scribe. No parchment. Just a single coin left behind, rolling lazily in a draft from the Loggia.

A grizzled merchant beside him spat. “They’ve gone. All of them. Even the ones who should be here.”

Lorenzo’s fingers twitched. He picked up the coin, flipped it in his palm. A *ducato*. Medici mint. “Where?”

The merchant jerked his chin toward the back of the market. “Palazzo. They’re sealing the vaults. Saying something’s wrong with the ledgers.”

Lorenzo pocketed the coin. “What ledgers?”

The merchant’s eyes flicked over him, dismissive. “Yours, isn’t it? Strozzi’s ghost bank. Everyone knows you’re the one who moves Medici gold when they don’t want their fingers dirty.”

Lorenzo said nothing.

The merchant leaned in, voice dropping. “I heard the Pazzi girl’s been asking after you. Not in the market. In the church. San Marco.”

Lorenzo’s jaw tightened. He turned toward the Church of San Marco, its rough stone blocks baking in the sun. “When?”

The merchant shrugged. “This morning. Early mass.”

A bell tolled from the cathedral. Not noon yet. Still time.

Lorenzo moved through the thinning crowd, past stalls selling dried herbs and leather boots, past the *bancarelle* where the *sensali* whispered of collapsing wool prices. At the edge of the market, near the *Pescia* fountain, he spotted a familiar face leaning against a buttress, arms crossed, face half-hidden in shadow.

Poliziano.

Dressed as a merchant, but his hands were too clean, his boots too soft. He didn’t look up as Lorenzo approached.

“You made your move,” Poliziano said.

“Yes.”

“You emptied the guild ledgers.”

“Yes.”

Poliziano finally looked at him. “You don’t sound proud.”

Lorenzo wiped sweat from his brow with the back of his hand. “Pride’s a luxury. I need gold.”

“And you’ve just made every guild master in Florence your enemy.”

“Good.” Lorenzo adjusted the frayed collar of his cloak. “Enemies are easier to count than friends when the walls start shaking.”

Poliziano pushed off the buttress, stepping into the sunlight. “The treasurer’s screaming. The Medici stewards are in a frenzy. You’ve pulled the plug on their liquidity.”

Lorenzo walked past him, toward the church steps. “Let them scream into their pillows.”

Poliziano grabbed his arm. “They’re looking for you.”

Lorenzo shook him off. “Let them.”

Poliziano’s voice dropped. “Lorenzo. The Pazzi girl’s inside. And she’s not alone.”

Lorenzo paused. “Who with?”

“Savonarola.”

The name settled like a stone in his gut. He turned toward the church doors. Through the arched entry, he saw the flicker of candlelight, the murmur of voices.

Poliziano followed his gaze. “You can’t go in there. Not like this. Not now.”

Lorenzo didn’t answer. He adjusted the cloak one last time, making sure the hood obscured his face. The fabric smelled of damp wool and old sweat. Not Medici. Not *his*. But it would do.

He stepped onto the shaded portico.

Poliziano’s hand clamped onto his shoulder. “Lorenzo, wait—”

He shook it off and pushed through the heavy oak doors.

The Church of San Marco smelled of incense and old stone. Sunlight filtered through the stained-glass windows, painting the nave in fractured hues—cobalt and ruby, gold and green. A handful of worshippers knelt in prayer. Near the altar, a cluster of men stood in quiet conversation: a monk in dark robes, a young woman in a dark dress with a white wimple, and beside her, Fra Savonarola himself, his gaunt face lit by the flicker of candlelight.

Contessina Pazzi.

She wasn’t looking at the preacher. She was looking at the ledgers stacked on a side table—medici ledgers, their covers stamped with the Medici palle. She held one open, her fingers tracing the columns of figures.

Lorenzo’s pulse thickened. He knew those ledgers. Every page. Every column. Every fraud.

Poliziano had warned him. *She knows.*

But this wasn’t knowledge. This was *proof.*

Contessina turned slightly, her profile sharp against the stained glass. Her hand rested on the ledger like it belonged there.

Savonarola said something in a low voice. Contessina didn’t answer. She lifted her gaze and met Lorenzo’s.

For a heartbeat, neither moved.

Then she said, low and clear, audible even across the nave, “You’re early, Strozzi.”

The money-changer’s words echoed in his skull: *Even Strozzi would know this game.*

The monk turned. A Medici steward. His eyes locked onto Lorenzo.

Savonarola stopped mid-sentence.

Contessina’s fingers tightened on the ledger. She didn’t look away.

Lorenzo’s breath steadied. He stepped forward, boots echoing on the stone.

The steward moved toward him, hand going to his dagger. “You—”

Lorenzo raised both hands, palms open. “Peace, messer. I come in peace.”

The steward wasn’t listening. His blade whispered from its sheath.

Contessina’s voice cut through the tension. “Wait.”

The steward froze.

She closed the ledger with a sharp slap. “Let him speak.”

The steward hesitated, then lowered his dagger slightly, confused.

Lorenzo walked forward, slow and deliberate, every step a calculation. The ledger in Contessina’s hands was open to a page he recognized. The Bruges branch. The Naples loan. His handwriting, disguised as his father’s.

She had found his ledgers.

And she was showing them to Savonarola.

Lorenzo stopped three paces away. “Contessina.”

She didn’t smile. “Lorenzo.”

Savonarola’s eyes shifted between them, sharp and knowing. “You know this man?”

Contessina didn’t answer. She lifted the ledger, turning it toward Lorenzo. “This line,” she said. “The Naples loan. It’s dated six months before my father died.”

The steward’s eyes darted to the date, then to Lorenzo.

Lorenzo said nothing.

Contessina flipped the page. Another ledger. Florence’s wool guild. Another date. Another loan.

Another lie.

“All Medici,” she said. “All fake.”

The steward’s grip tightened on his dagger. “This is the Strozzi banker.”

Contessina’s gaze didn’t waver. “He’s not a banker.”

The steward lunged.

Lorenzo sidestepped, but the steward was fast. The dagger slashed air where his throat had been. Lorenzo twisted, grabbing the ledger from Contessina’s hands. She didn’t resist.

The steward swung again. Lorenzo ducked, felt the blade graze his shoulder. Pain flared, hot and bright.

Then Poliziano was there, shoving the steward back, his voice a low growl. “Enough.”

The steward snarled, but Poliziano’s presence—tall, unyielding—held him.

Lorenzo pressed a hand to his bleeding shoulder. He kept the ledger clutched in his other hand.

Contessina’s face was pale. “They’ll kill you for this.”

Lorenzo looked at her. “Not if they need what’s in this ledger.”

She stared at him, eyes dark with something he couldn’t name—anger, pity, recognition.

Then she did the last thing he expected.

She stepped forward and pressed something cold and hard into his palm.

A key.

“You’ll need this,” she said, voice barely above a whisper. “San Marco’s crypt. The third niche from the left. Everything’s there.”

His fingers closed around the key.

The steward spat on the floor. “Traitor.”

Poliziano grabbed Lorenzo’s arm. “We have to go. Now.”

Lorenzo didn’t move. He looked at Contessina. “Why?”

Her lips thinned. “Because I know what you’re doing. And I want to help.”

Before he could answer, Poliziano yanked him toward the door. “Not here. Not now.”

Lorenzo let himself be pulled, but his eyes never left hers.

Contessina held his gaze until the heavy oak doors swung shut behind him, cutting off the dim church light.

Outside, the sun blazed. Poliziano dragged him into the alley, breathing hard. “You’re bleeding.”

Lorenzo touched his shoulder. The blood was warm, sticky. “It’s shallow.”

Poliziano’s voice was a whip. “You just handed a Pazzi girl Medici ledgers in front of Savonarola and a Medici steward. You’ve just made every enemy in Florence *your* enemy.”

Lorenzo wiped his hand on his cloak, leaving a smear of red. He looked at the key in his palm.

It gleamed dully in the sunlight.

Poliziano followed his gaze. “What’s that?”

“A key to the crypt,” Lorenzo said.

Poliziano’s face darkened. “To what?”

Lorenzo pocketed the key. “To the truth.”

Poliziano grabbed his wrist, fingers pressing hard into the wound. “You’re hurt. You’re wanted. And you just gave a Pazzi the means to destroy you.”

Lorenzo pulled free. “She already knows.”

Poliziano exhaled sharply. “God help us. You’re playing with fire.”

Lorenzo looked back at the church doors, half-expecting to see Savonarola leading a mob, or Contessina with a dagger of her own.

But the portico was empty.

Only silence remained.

He turned toward the Mercato Nuovo, where the money-changers’ empty tables still stood like tombstones. Somewhere in the distance, a bell tolled.

Poliziano kept pace beside him. “Where to?”

Lorenzo wiped the blood from his fingers on his sleeve. “To the next move.”

Poliziano’s voice dropped. “And what move is that?”

Lorenzo didn’t answer.

He stepped into the blazing sun, the key heavy in his pocket, the wound on his shoulder stinging like a brand.

The ledgers were in Contessina’s hands.

And Florence was burning.

Chapter 8

The Palazzo Medici smelled of lavender and sickness.

Lorenzo stood in the dim corridor outside Clarice’s chambers, the flicker of oil lamps painting the frescoed walls in gold and crimson. The usual bustle of servants had been replaced by near-silence, broken only by the murmur of hushed words and the shuffle of slippered feet. A physician’s coat—a rich crimson, embroidered with gold thread—lay abandoned on a side table, the sleeves still damp with sweat.

Poliziano leaned against the wall beside him, arms crossed. His usual poise was frayed at the edges. “She hasn’t eaten in two days,” he said. “The physicians are baffled.”

Lorenzo exhaled through his nose. “They’re charlatans. She’s been poisoned.”

Poliziano didn’t argue. He didn’t need to. They both knew the signs—the dark circles under Clarice’s eyes, the way her breath came too fast, the unnatural stillness of her hands on the coverlet.

A nurse emerged from the chamber, her face gaunt with exhaustion. She curtsied to Poliziano, then to Lorenzo, though her eyes lingered on him a second too long. “Messer Poliziano, she asks for you.”

Poliziano nodded and stepped past Lorenzo. The nurse hesitated, then followed.

Lorenzo didn’t move. He watched Poliziano disappear into the chamber, heard the rustle of fabric as the door fell shut behind them. Then silence.

He turned and walked down the corridor, past the portraits of dead Medici, their painted eyes seeming to follow him. The air grew heavier the farther he went, thick with the scent of illness and incense. Somewhere, a priest prayed in Latin, the words rolling like smoke through the stone halls.

He stopped at an arched window, pressing his palm against the cool glass. Beyond the courtyard, Florence stretched out—a sprawl of terracotta roofs and the distant hum of the city. The Mercato Vecchio was visible, its stalls half-empty, the guilds whispering of collapse. The Medici Bank’s foreign branches were failing. Bruges had frozen their credit. Cairo’s agents had vanished with Medici gold. And now, in Florence, the church bells tolled for another reason.

Not for mass.

For Clarice.

A cough echoed from within the chamber—dry, rattling. Not hers. Poliziano’s voice rose in response, low and urgent. Lorenzo could not hear the words, but he knew the tone. Pleading. Reassuring.

He closed his eyes.

He had done this.

Not directly. Not with poison in a cup or a blade in the dark. But he had starved the Medici of liquidity. He had bled the branches dry. He had made the bank cough blood.

And now the wife of the Magnificent was coughing too.

A hand touched his shoulder.

He turned.

Fra Savonarola stood there, his dark robes blending into the shadows. His face was gaunt, his eyes burning with the fervor of a man who had seen visions and refused to look away.

“Messer Strozzi,” the friar said. His voice was soft, almost gentle.

Lorenzo said nothing.

Savonarola stepped closer, close enough that Lorenzo could smell the incense clinging to his sleeves. “You walk these halls as if you belong.”

“I once did.”

“But no longer?”

Lorenzo looked past him, toward the chamber where Poliziano still soothed the dying woman. “I belong where the ledgers bleed.”

Savonarola’s lips twitched. “The bank is not the body of Christ.”

“No. But it bleeds just the same.”

The friar’s fingers tightened around the wooden cross hanging from his neck. “You think this is power? To watch the mighty fall?”

“I think it’s justice.”

Savonarola leaned in, his breath warm on Lorenzo’s cheek. “Then you serve a false god.”

Lorenzo turned fully toward him. “And you serve a god who starves the poor so the Medici can build palaces.”

The friar’s jaw tightened. “Florence is corrupt.”

“So is your god, if he allows it.”

Savonarola’s hand lashed out, gripping Lorenzo’s wrist. His fingers were cold, skeletal. “You tempt me, Strozzi. A man who plays with fire should fear the flame.”

Lorenzo didn’t pull away. “I’m not afraid.”

“You should be.” Savonarola released him abruptly. “The Magnificent’s wife lies dying. His enemies gather. And you stand here, smug as a serpent in Eden.”

Lorenzo flexed his fingers, the imprint of the friar’s grip still sharp. “I have no serpent’s pride.”

“Then what do you have?”

“Patience.”

Savonarola’s gaze flicked over him, dismissive. “Patience is a virtue of the weak.”

“And strength is the illusion of the desperate.” Lorenzo stepped past him. “Good day, Fra Savonarola.”

The friar didn’t move. “You think you are the spider in the web? Pulling threads unseen?”

Lorenzo paused at the mouth of the corridor. “I know I am.”

“Then we shall see whose web holds.” Savonarola’s voice followed him like smoke. “The Pazzi girl seeks you. She whispers in San Marco. The stewards hunt you. The bank bleeds. And now the Magnificent’s wife—”

Lorenzo stopped. Turned.

Savonarola’s smile was slow, triumphant. “She named you before the priest. *Lorenzo Strozzi.* The ghost banker. The Medici’s shadow.”

Lorenzo’s pulse steadied. “And?”

“And the Magnificent knows.”

A silence fell, thick as the incense in the air. Lorenzo looked down the corridor, past the sickbed chamber, toward the grand staircase where Medici guards patrolled, their armor gleaming in the lamplight.

He had expected this. Had prepared for it. But not here. Not now. Not while Clarice still drew breath.

Savonarola tilted his head. “You look surprised.”

“I look wary.”

“Wary is wise.” The friar stepped back, melting into the shadows. “But wisdom comes too late for fools.”

Lorenzo watched him go, the hem of the friar’s robes whispering against the stone. Then he turned and walked toward the chamber where Poliziano stood guard over the wife of his enemy.

The door creaked open as he approached.

Poliziano stood inside, his face pale with exhaustion. He didn’t speak.

Lorenzo stepped past him.

Clarice lay propped against the pillows, her skin sallow, her dark hair spread across the linen like ink on parchment. Her eyes opened as he entered, heavy-lidded but aware.

“Lorenzo,” she whispered.

He stopped at the foot of the bed. The physicians had been right about one thing—she was dying. But not from poison. From something worse.

Hope.

“You should rest,” he said.

She tried to smile. It was weak, pained. “I have rested enough.”

Poliziano moved to her side, pouring water from a silver jug into a goblet. “Drink, madonna.”

She shook her head. “Not water.”

Lorenzo’s gaze locked onto her. “What do you want?”

Her lips parted. “The truth.”

Poliziano’s hand stilled over the goblet.

Lorenzo didn’t look away from Clarice. “You already suspect it.”

“Not suspect.” Her voice was a thread. “*Know.*”

A cough racked her frame. She clenched her teeth, then relaxed again. “You took from them. Every branch. Every ledger. You made them weak.”

Poliziano set the goblet down. “Clarice—”

“Let him speak, Poliziano.” Her eyes never left Lorenzo’s. “You’re the reason my husband’s hands shake when he signs decrees. You’re the reason the Bruges branch weeps. You’re the reason Florence whispers *traitor* when he walks by.”

Lorenzo said nothing.

Clarice’s hand twitched on the coverlet. “You did it for your father.”

A muscle in Lorenzo’s jaw flexed. “Yes.”

“And now you watch her die.”

Poliziano made a sharp sound—half gasp, half protest.

Lorenzo didn’t flinch. “I didn’t touch the wine. The physician did.”

“But you knew.” Her voice was a blade, dull but certain. “You always know.”

The silence between them was a living thing, twisting in the air. Poliziano’s breath hitched. Clarice’s gaze softened, just slightly.

Then she lifted her hand.

A vial lay in her palm, small and delicate, stoppered with gold leaf. The Medici crest was etched into the glass.

Lorenzo’s breath caught.

He recognized it.

He had given it to her himself—weeks ago, in a garden, when she had begged for something to ease her melancholy. A tonic. Something to sleep.

But the gold leaf had been his final warning.

Poliziano saw it too. His face went gray.

Clarice’s fingers trembled as she held the vial toward Lorenzo. “I took it last night. Thought it would help me dream of something other than this.” She gestured weakly to the room, the illness, the suffocating air. “But when I opened it, the liquid was black.”

Lorenzo didn’t move.

Poliziano stepped forward. “Clarice, don’t—”

She uncorked the vial with a sharp *pop*.

A waft of bitter almonds filled the air.

Cyanide.

Lorenzo’s blood turned to ice.

Clarice lifted the vial to her lips.

Poliziano lunged, knocking the vial from her hand. It shattered on the stone floor, the black liquid pooling like oil, the gold leaf glittering in the lamplight.

Clarice gasped, clutching her throat. Her face twisted in pain—real pain, not the sickness of the illness, but the searing burn of poison.

Lorenzo stood frozen.

Poliziano dropped to his knees beside the shattered vial, his hands shaking as he reached for her. “Clarice!”

She coughed, a wet, choking sound. Blood flecked her lips.

Lorenzo finally moved.

He crossed the room in three strides, grabbed Poliziano’s arm, and yanked him away. Poliziano snarled, but Lorenzo’s grip was iron.

“She’s gone,” Lorenzo said, voice low and urgent. “Don’t touch her. Don’t let anyone touch her.”

Poliziano’s eyes widened. “She’s *poisoned*—”

“No.” Lorenzo’s voice was a rasp. “She’s *dead.*”

Clarice’s body convulsed once. Then went still.

Poliziano’s breath came in ragged bursts. He stared at the body, then at Lorenzo. “You—”

Lorenzo released him and stepped back. His eyes burned into Poliziano’s. A warning. A promise.

Then he turned and walked out of the chamber, leaving the friar’s chants and the physician’s wails behind him.

He moved down the hall, past the Medici portraits, past the guards who bowed without meeting his eyes.

He did not run.

He did not look back.

When he reached the grand staircase, he paused, his hand resting on the balustrade.

Somewhere in the palace, a bell began to toll.

Not for grief.

For war.

Chapter 9

The olive trees in the Palazzo Medici garden stood gnarled and silver, their leaves whispering against the stone wall. The late afternoon sun slanted through the branches, painting the ground in stripes of green and gold. Lorenzo sat on a marble bench, one hand resting on the ledger tucked beneath his cloak. The weight of it pressed into his thigh like a promise.

A boot crunched on gravel.

He didn’t look up.

Poliziano emerged from the shadows, his red doublet immaculate, his face unreadable. He carried a single parchment, rolled tight in his gloved hand.

“You’ve been avoiding the Magnificent,” Poliziano said.

Lorenzo plucked a dried leaf from the bench, rolled it between his fingers. “I’ve been busy.”

“With what?”

“Surviving.”

Poliziano stopped a step away. “He wants you in the inner circle.”

That got his attention.

Lorenzo raised his gaze. Poliziano’s eyes were dark, intense. The man had aged since Clarice’s death—not in his bearing, but in the set of his jaw, the tension in his shoulders. Like a lute string drawn too tight.

“He said that?” Lorenzo asked.

Poliziano unrolled the parchment. A formal appointment scroll, sealed with the Medici crest. “Counselor to the Republic. First voice in fiscal matters. Equal voice in governance.”

Lorenzo didn’t move. “Equal to whom?”

“To those who remain.”

A bee hummed past, its wings a blur against the sun. Lorenzo exhaled through his nose. “And what does Florence get?”

“Stability. Control. A man who understands the ledgers better than anyone.” Poliziano stepped closer, the parchment rustling. “You.”

Lorenzo plucked the scroll from Poliziano’s hand, unsealed it with a flick of his thumbnail. The wax crackled. He didn’t read the words. He knew the shape of power when he saw it. “And what do *you* get?”

Poliziano’s fingers twitched. “Permission to speak to a man before he burns the city down.”

Lorenzo let the scroll roll closed. “I don’t burn cities.”

“No. You starve them. You bleed them. You make them whisper *traitor* when men like de’ Pazzi walk past.” Poliziano’s voice dropped. “You make wives beg for death in their sickbeds.”

The ledger under Lorenzo’s cloak felt suddenly hot.

He stood. The scroll dangled from his fingers. “You’ve been reading the wrong ledgers, Poliziano.”

“No. I’ve been reading the *right* ones.” Poliziano’s gaze flicked over him, not with judgment, but with something sharper. Fear. “The ones you hide. The ones that trace every ghost loan, every shell account, back to the Pazzi gold seized after ’78.”

Lorenzo tossed the scroll onto the bench. It landed with a soft *thud*. “You think I care what you know?”

“I think you care what *Venice* knows. What *Naples* knows. What *Rome* will know when the ledgers surface in the right hands.” Poliziano stepped into the sunlight, his face half in shadow. “I think you care that your name is carved into every fraud, every embezzlement, every secret Medici crime.”

A breeze stirred the olive leaves. Lorenzo felt it on his neck, cool against the heat rising in his chest.

Poliziano wasn’t done. “He’s offering you legitimacy, Lorenzo. A voice. A shield. You could walk these halls not as a ghost, but as the man who saved Florence from itself.”

“By what cost?” Lorenzo asked.

“By ending this.”

“This?” Lorenzo gestured between them. “The game? The revenge? The slow unraveling of a system built on theft and lies?”

Poliziano’s jaw tightened. “Yes.”

“And if I refuse?”

Poliziano didn’t flinch. “Then you’re the enemy.”

The air between them turned electric. Lorenzo could feel the weight of the moments stacked here—Clarice’s poison, the dying bank, the tolling bells, the ledgers bleeding secrets into the dark.

He picked the scroll back up. “And if I accept?”

Poliziano exhaled. “Then you help him rebuild.”

Lorenzo unrolled the parchment again. His eyes traced the words, the formal language, the Medici promise. It smelled of ink and power and old paper.

He looked up.

Poliziano was watching him, waiting.

Lorenzo smiled. Not the cautious smile of a banker, but something sharper. A blade unsheathed.

“I prefer the shadows,” he said.

Poliziano’s face went still.

Lorenzo let the scroll fall from his fingers. It fluttered to the ground like a dead thing.

Poliziano didn’t move to pick it up.

Lorenzo stepped past him, brushing his shoulder as he went. Poliziano’s breath caught. Not in collision, but in realization.

Lorenzo didn’t glance back.

He walked through the garden, past the gnarled trees, toward the distant murmur of the Mercato Nuovo. The sun dipped lower, casting long shadows behind him.

Behind, Poliziano stood motionless, his hand hovering near the scroll on the ground.

His fingers trembled.

Not from cold.

From defeat.

Chapter 10

The room smelled of vellum and beeswax. Candles burned low in iron sconces, their flames stretched long by a draft creeping through a cracked window in the north wall. Outside, the clatter of hooves on cobblestone from Via dei Neri drifted up, but in here, there was only the scratch of quills and the rustle of ledgers.

Lorenzo stood between two scribes—brothers, both from Lucca, both loyal to the money, not the name. Their desks were covered in open volumes, pages spread wide like wings torn from birds. The brothers worked in silence most nights, but tonight their fingers trembled.

Giannozzo, the elder, looked up from a ledger marked *Secretum*. “You want it all?”

Lorenzo didn’t answer. He laid the last volume across Giannozzo’s desk. The spine cracked. Inside, every Medici loan for a decade, traced through shell accounts, names erased, numbers re-inked, fortunes diverted.

Giannozzo’s eyes flickered over the entries. His brother, Matteo, stopped mid-stroke, quill hovering.

“You’ve been doing this for years,” Matteo said.

“Yes.”

Giannozzo closed the book gently. “This isn’t banking. This is assassination.”

Lorenzo pulled a second ledger from his satchel. “This one’s older. Pazzi assets, reallocated after ’78. The gold never went to the Signoria. It went to shell accounts in Bruges and London.”

Matteo’s face paled. “You’ve been rebuilding the Pazzi fortune?”

“No,” Lorenzo said. “I’ve been *repossessing* it.”

Giannozzo exhaled through his nose. “And the interest?”

“Compound. Reinvested. Hidden.” Lorenzo tapped the ledger with one finger. “The Medici Bank didn’t stand on genius. It stood on theft. And now I hold the proof.”

The brothers exchanged a glance. Not fear—respect. Or maybe calculation. Men who measured value in coins understood leverage.

Matteo set his quill down. “What do you want us to do with this?”

“Organize it. Page by page. Transaction by transaction. Every bribe, every embezzled *lira*, every loan that never existed but did anyway.” Lorenzo leaned forward. “I want it bound in red leather. Stamped with the Strozzi crest. Left on the desk of Lorenzo de’ Medici before the Feast of the Annunciation.”

Giannozzo’s fingers drummed the desk. “And after?”

“After,” Lorenzo said, “Florence asks who made them starve.”

A gust of wind rattled the shutters. The candle between them guttered, the flame diving like a wounded bird before steadying.

Giannozzo picked up a fresh sheet of vellum. “We start with the Naples ledger. The one from ’85.”

Matteo unrolled a scroll of blank pages. “And the Bruges transfers?”

“All of them.” Lorenzo pulled a small iron key from his belt. “This opens the strongbox under the Mercato Nuovo. There’s a chest inside. Inside that, the original Pazzi ledgers. Burn the copies. Bring me the originals.”

The brothers didn’t ask why. They knew why.

Giannozzo took the key, turned it in his palm. “When the city starves, they’ll ask who siphoned the grain.”

“Let them,” Lorenzo said. “I’ll make sure they have names.”

Matteo dipped his quill into ink. “And the interest on the interest?”

Lorenzo smiled. Not because it amused him, but because it was the only honest expression in the room.

“That,” he said, “is what will topple them.”

Giannozzo uncapped a bottle of glue. He laid a spine strip across the ledger, smoothed it with his thumb. “You’re building a gallows, Lorenzo.”

“A court of inquiry,” Lorenzo corrected. “With eyes on every ledger in Florence.”

Matteo wet his finger, flicked through a page. His breath hitched. “Here. The Florence militia payroll. Paid twice a month to phantom officers.”

Lorenzo reached over, flipped the pages. His eyes moved down the columns. Forged signatures. Double entries. Men who existed on paper only, drawing wages while real soldiers went unpaid.

He closed the ledger. “Burn the list of names. But keep the totals. Let the city wonder why their taxes paid for mercenaries who never marched.”

The brothers worked in silence again, but the air hummed with it. Not fear, not rebellion—understanding. Men who knew the weight of a ledger understood the weight of revenge.

Lorenzo watched them for a moment, then turned toward the door.

“Where are you going?” Matteo asked without looking up.

“To see Contessina.”

Giannozzo’s quill paused. “Pazzi?”

Lorenzo didn’t answer. He didn’t have to.

When he pushed through the heavy oak door, the night air bit into his skin. Torches burned in brackets along the street, casting long shadows on the stones. The Mercato Nuovo was quiet now—no merchants, no moneychangers, just the occasional rat skittering over cobblestones.

He walked past the closed shutters of the Strozzi Bank ruins, past the guildhall where Medici coins had once bought silence. His boots made no sound on the damp pavement.

The Pazzi palace loomed ahead, its windows dark but not empty. He knew she would be there. Always awake after midnight. Always watching.

He knocked once. The door opened before the echo faded.

Contessina stood in the dim light of a single oil lamp, her black dress stark against the warm tones of the hall. No jewelry. No veil. Just a woman in mourning who had long since stopped pretending.

She didn’t speak. Didn’t ask who it was. She knew.

Lorenzo stepped inside. The door closed behind him with a soft *click*.

She turned, her profile sharp in the lamplight. “You have the proof.”

Not a question.

“Yes.”

“You’re going to use it.”

Another statement.

“Yes.”

“To destroy them.”

He paused. Not because he hesitated, but because the words hung in the air like smoke.

“To expose them,” he said. “To let Florence see what they’ve done.”

She stepped closer. Her gaze didn’t waver. “And then?”

“Then I let the city decide.”

A ghost of a smile touched her lips. Not kindness. Recognition.

“You’re not a Medici,” she said. “You’re not a banker. You’re the man who took their ledgers and used them against them.”

“And you?” he asked. “What are you?”

For the first time, something flickered in her eyes. Not grief. Something older. Hunger.

“Their ghost,” she said. “Waiting in the dark. And now, so are you.”

The lamp flickered. The draft from the courtyard found them again, tugging at the edges of their silence.

She reached out, not touching him, but close enough that he felt the warmth of her skin.

“What will you do when they ask who siphoned the grain?” she asked.

Lorenzo looked at her, at the woman who had lost her family to the same ledgers he now held.

“Tell them the truth,” he said. “That the Medici didn’t just rule Florence.”

He stepped back.

“They bled it.”

Chapter 11

The Palazzo Medici’s private chapel was empty of supplicants. Dust hung in the slanted light from the high lancet windows, motes spiraling like the ghosts of forgotten prayers. Lorenzo de’ Medici stood with his back to the entrance, shoulders rigid under his crimson doublet, gazing up at the new fresco that covered the east wall. It was finished. Or nearly so.

He hadn’t expected Botticelli to go so far.

The central figure shone like a fallen star—Lorenzo de’ Medici, not as patron, but as divine presence, his hand outstretched to the heavens. Around him, Florence itself knelt: merchants counting coins, scholars bending over scrolls, artisans shaping stone. But in the background, half-hidden behind a pillar of lapis lazuli, a shadow stretched—lean, menacing, unmistakable. A man in a dark cloak, fingers curled as if pulling invisible strings.

Lorenzo de’ Medici exhaled through his nose, cold and controlled. “He’s gone too far.”

A door creaked behind him. He didn’t turn. He didn’t need to.

“Too far?” Agnolo Poliziano’s voice was soft, measured. “Or too honest?”

Lorenzo didn’t move. “Art should uplift, not accuse.”

Poliziano stepped closer, his scholar’s robes whispering against the marble floor. “Then Botticelli chose his god. And his devil.”

Lorenzo finally turned, his face unreadable. “You sound as if you approve.”

“I sound as if I see what others won’t.” Poliziano gestured toward the fresco. “That shadow isn’t a demon, my lord. It’s a mirror.”

A flicker of irritation crossed Lorenzo’s face. “You always cloak cowardice in poetry.”

Poliziano didn’t flinch. “And you cloak tyranny in mercy.”

Silence settled between them, thick as incense smoke. Outside, a mule brayed in the courtyard. A servant’s slippered step echoed in the corridor, then faded.

Lorenzo tilted his head. “Where’s the artist?”

“In his studio,” Poliziano said.

“Still alive?”

“At last report.”

Lorenzo exhaled sharply. “Good. I’d rather not mourn another man today.”

Poliziano watched him closely. “You look like a man who’s just seen his own funeral bier.”

Lorenzo stepped toward the fresco again, his shadow long and sharp against the painted stone. “I’ve seen death before. In ledgers, in signatures, in the hollow eyes of men who signed their lives away for our favor.” He raised a hand, not touching the wall, but hovering over the shadowed figure. “This isn’t death. It’s *defiance*.”

Poliziano joined him. “You fear defiance.”

“No,” Lorenzo said quietly. “I *understand* it. Botticelli painted what he saw. Not in Florence—*in the bank*.” He turned, eyes dark. “Someone’s been rewriting the Medici account. Not from above. From below.”

Poliziano’s throat tightened. “That’s preposterous.”

“Is it?” Lorenzo’s voice was calm, deadly. “Then explain the Naples transfer. Explain the Bruges discrepancy. Explain why the Strozzi name keeps appearing in cancelled contracts.”

Poliziano opened his mouth, then closed it. He knew. He’d known for weeks. But knowing and admitting were different things.

“You suspect someone close,” Poliziano said carefully.

“Someone whose hands I once shook.” Lorenzo’s gaze darkened. “Someone who knows every ledger better than I do.”

A knock at the chapel door. Not a servant’s—too firm, too deliberate.

Both men turned.

Contessina Pazzi stepped inside, cloaked in black, her face pale as candle wax. She didn’t bow. She didn’t curtsy. She simply looked at Lorenzo with eyes that had seen bloodshed and ruin.

“You’ve seen the fresco,” she said.

Lorenzo inclined his head. “And so have you.”

“Not just seen,” she said. “Recognized.”

Lorenzo felt Poliziano stiffen beside him.

Contessina moved forward, her boots clicking on the marble. She stopped just beyond the reach of the light, her shadow falling fully on the fresco. “That figure—it’s not a warning. It’s a signature.”

Poliziano’s breath hitched. “Contessina—”

“Don’t.” She cut him off. “I know what you think. That this is about revenge. About debt. About old scores.” She stepped closer to Lorenzo. “But look at the date. *Kalendis Aprilis*.”

Lorenzo’s jaw tightened.

“April first,” she continued. “Easter. The anniversary of the Pazzi Conspiracy. The day our family bled in the cathedral.” Her voice dropped. “The day your family began to rise.”

Poliziano exhaled sharply. “You’re saying he’s announcing it.”

Lorenzo didn’t answer. He didn’t have to.

Contessina met his gaze. “You’re not just rewriting ledgers, Lorenzo. You’re rewriting history. And Botticelli put it in paint where every man in Florence can see.”

A gust of wind rattled the chapel’s stained-glass window. The painted light trembled on the floor.

Poliziano stepped forward. “Lorenzo, control yourself. This isn’t—”

“This isn’t what?” Lorenzo turned, slow and deliberate. “This isn’t the truth? This isn’t what’s already happening in the shadows of the Mercato Nuovo? This isn’t the ledger page already circulating in the guild halls?” He let the words hang. “Then explain to me why Botticelli is dead in his studio. Why his final sketch shows the same shadow, clutching a ledger stained with his own blood.”

Contessina paled. Poliziano’s face went ashen.

Lorenzo stepped between them, his voice low. “They’re coming for me. And I’m ready.”

A long silence. Then Poliziano spoke, barely above a whisper. “You did this.”

Lorenzo didn’t deny it.

Contessina’s fingers twitched at her sides. “You killed a genius because he saw what you are.”

“No,” Lorenzo said. “I killed a man who saw what I’ve become. And now he’s silent.”

Poliziano staggered back as if struck. “God’s teeth…”

Contessina’s voice was a blade. “You’re no banker, Lorenzo. You’re a murderer.”

Lorenzo looked at her, truly looked at her, for the first time in months. Not as the woman who hated the Medici. Not as the heiress of a ruined house. But as a woman who had lost everything—and now saw the architect of that ruin standing before her.

He reached into his doublet. Poliziano tensed. Contessina didn’t move.

Lorenzo pulled out a folded sheet of vellum. He held it out—not to either of them, but to the fresco itself.

Then he let it fall.

The paper fluttered to the floor, landing face-up.

A ledger line, stained with crimson.

Across the bottom, in elegant script: *Strozzi Revenans.*

Poliziano bent and picked it up. His hands shook.

Contessina didn’t look at it. She looked at Lorenzo.

And in that moment, something broke.

Not in her. In the world.

The chapel’s door groaned open behind them.

Lorenzo’s eyes flicked to it.

A guard stood there, halberd resting on the floor, breath ragged.

“My lord,” he gasped. “The Strozzi brothers… they’ve been found in the Mercato Nuovo. Their throats cut. Their ledgers burned.”

Silence.

Then Lorenzo moved.

He strode past Poliziano and Contessina, his cloak whipping behind him like a flag of war.

Poliziano reached for him. “Lorenzo—”

“Stay out of this,” Lorenzo said over his shoulder. “Or join them in the dark.”

He didn’t wait for an answer.

The guard stepped aside. Lorenzo passed through the doorway, and the guard didn’t follow.

Poliziano and Contessina stood frozen, the weight of the vellum in his hand, the echo of blood in the air.

Contessina turned to Poliziano, her voice hollow. “He’s not just erasing ledgers anymore.”

Poliziano didn’t answer. He was reading the ledger line again.

*To the Medici Bank, from the Schatten Account: 200,000 florins. For services rendered in the name of Florence.*

And beneath it, a name.

One he knew.

One he had trusted.

The realization hit him like a knife between the ribs.

Lorenzo Strozzi.

Not the Strozzi.

*Never* the Strozzi.

Poliziano’s fingers closed around the paper until it crumpled.

Contessina touched his arm. “We have to stop him.”

Poliziano looked at her, eyes wide with horror.

“No,” he whispered. “We have to *save* him.”

From the stairwell, a slow, rhythmic clapping began.

Not from below.

From above.

From the chapel’s balcony, where a figure in black stood watching them, arms crossed.

Lorenzo de’ Medici smiled.

And then he spoke.

“Ah, Poliziano. I didn’t expect you to *share* my secrets.”

Chapter 12

The private chapel was too small for three men who no longer trusted each other. Lorenzo de’ Medici stood at the altar, his fingers tracing the edge of a carved angel’s wing. The fresco behind him pulsed with color—God’s hand descending, the Medici lion rampant, and in the corner, a shadow that watched from behind a lapis pillar.

Poliziano leaned against the doorframe, his scholar’s robes too heavy for the heat of the afternoon. He held the ledger page like a dead bird.

Lorenzo didn’t turn. “You’ve had it all this time.”

“And you’ve known I had it,” Poliziano replied.

A flicker of irritation crossed Lorenzo’s face. “You should have brought it to me sooner.”

“Should I?” Poliziano stepped forward, vellum crinkling in his grip. “Or should I have asked you why your handwriting is on the Strozzi Revenans ledger? Why every fraudulent transfer in the last twenty years bears the mark of a man who was supposed to be dead?”

Contessina Pazzi watched from the corner, silent as a blade in its sheath. She had not spoken since Lorenzo entered the chapel. Not a word, not a gasp, not a prayer. Only the rise and fall of her chest betrayed that she was still alive.

Lorenzo finally faced them. His eyes were calm. “The Strozzi Revenans ledger is a fabrication. A forgery. A trick to discredit the Medici name.”

Poliziano unfolded the page. “Then explain the matching entry in the Bruges ledger. The one you ‘corrected’ three days ago.”

A muscle twitched in Lorenzo’s jaw. “Bruges keeps poor records.”

“And the Naples transfer?” Poliziano pressed. “The one that saved your war chest after the French withdrew their support? The one that came from an account that didn’t exist until six years ago?”

Lorenzo exhaled through his nose. “You’re twisting words into swords.”

“No,” Poliziano said. “I’m twisting ledgers into mirrors.”

Contessina moved forward, her black skirts whispering against the marble. “You call it a forgery. I call it a confession.” She stopped two paces from Lorenzo. Close enough to smell the clove on his breath. Close enough to see the faint scar above his left eyebrow—the one he’d earned in the Pazzi riot. “Twenty years ago, my father was murdered in the Duomo. Twenty years ago, your family rose on his blood. And twenty years ago, a Strozzi infant was smuggled out of Florence in a wine cask.”

Lorenzo’s eyes didn’t waver. “You believe every orphan with a knife is a prince?”

“No,” she said. “I believe the man who carved April 1st on every forged ledger is the man who killed Botticelli.”

Poliziano flinched.

Lorenzo didn’t. “Botticelli died by his own hand. Grief over my brother’s death, the court whispers, the weight of patronage—any man would break under such pressure.”

“He was drawing your ledgers,” Contessina said. “He was going to paint them into the fresco. He was going to show Florence who really pulled the strings.”

“And now he’s dead,” Poliziano added quietly. “As are the Strozzi brothers. As is your father’s reputation.”

Lorenzo stepped toward the altar, hands clasped behind his back. “My father saved Florence from chaos. He brought stability. He—”

“He stole my family’s fortune,” Contessina cut in. “He bankrupted my father. He left us begging in the streets while your palaces grew taller.”

“And my family paid for it,” Lorenzo said softly. “Every Strozzi debt was called in. Every Strozzi asset seized. Every Strozzi name erased. All by the Medici Bank.”

Poliziano unfolded the vellum again. “Except one name wasn’t erased. It was reborn.”

A silence fell. Thick as incense, thick as guilt.

Lorenzo walked to the far wall, where the shadow in the fresco stared back at him. “If I were that man,” he said, voice low, “then who funded the loans that built the Duomo’s foundations? Who kept the grain ships sailing during the famine? Who paid the mercenaries that saved Florence from the French?”

“A ghost,” Poliziano answered.

Lorenzo turned slowly. “Ghosts don’t bleed.”

Contessina stepped forward. She reached into her sleeve and pulled out a second folded page. She did not offer it. She held it like a weapon. “This is your true ledger. The one you’ve used to rebuild what was taken.” She unfolded it with one hand, the other resting on the dagger at her belt. “One hundred thousand florins. Stolen from the Medici Bank. Invested in the shadow of the Mercato Nuovo. Repaid tenfold in interest. All in your name.”

Lorenzo’s gaze flicked to the page. Not in shock. Not in denial.

In recognition.

His lips parted. A breath escaped. Almost a laugh.

Poliziano watched him, horrified and fascinated. He had known Lorenzo for twenty years. Had shared wine, poetry, and midnight confessions. But this man—this mask—was not the boy he had tutored.

This was the architect of ruin.

Contessina did not lower the page. “You’re not Lorenzo Strozzi. You never were.”

Lorenzo’s fingers curled into fists. “And you,” he said, voice rough, “are not the proud Pazzi daughter I thought you were.”

She did not flinch. “I’m the woman who just proved you’re a murderer.”

“And you?” he asked, gaze flicking to Poliziano. “You, who swore to uphold truth and beauty—does this poem make sense now?”

Poliziano didn’t answer. He couldn’t. The words were lodged in his throat like stones.

Lorenzo stepped closer to Contessina. Close enough that she could have driven the dagger into his heart. He studied the ledger page, then her face, then the shadow in the fresco. Something shifted in his expression. Not fear. Not guilt.

Resolution.
