“You’re right,” he said softly. “I was never Strozzi. Not by blood. Not by name. Not by choice.”

Contessina’s grip tightened on the dagger. “Then who are you?”

Lorenzo met her eyes. His voice dropped to a whisper.

“Your king.”

Poliziano staggered back. “God’s blood—”

But Lorenzo wasn’t finished.

He reached out. Not to take the page. Not to strike. He touched the fresco above the shadowed figure—the one that looked like him, the one that looked like a god.

And he pressed his palm to the painted wall.

The pigment was still wet.

A thin line of red seeped beneath his fingers.

He smiled.

Then he turned to Poliziano.

“You always said I was a poet, Agnolo. A man of words and whispers. But poetry is just ledgers without numbers. And numbers don’t lie.”

Contessina lowered the page. “What are you doing?”

“Finishing what I started,” Lorenzo said. He walked past them both, toward the chapel door.

Poliziano grabbed his arm. “Lorenzo—stop. You’re confessing to treason.”

Lorenzo looked down at Poliziano’s hand. Not with anger. With pity.

“No,” he said. “I’m claiming what’s mine.”

He pulled free and strode into the corridor. His boots echoed against the stone.

Contessina followed, her skirts rustling. Poliziano rushed after them, vellum crumpled in his fist.

They emerged into the Palazzo Medici’s inner courtyard, where sunlight cut the air like a blade. Servants froze. Guards reached for halberds.

Lorenzo walked to the center of the courtyard, where a fountain trickled blood-red water into a stone basin. He climbed onto the rim.

Contessina and Poliziano stopped at the edge of the crowd.

Lorenzo raised the stained hand.

“Citizens of Florence!” His voice carried, clear and strong. “For twenty years, you have lived under the shadow of the Medici Bank. You have paid tithes to a family that bled your guilds dry. You have starved while palaces rose. You have knelt before men who called themselves gods.”

A murmur rippled through the courtyard.

“But today,” Lorenzo said, “I lift the veil. The Medici Bank is a fraud. The loans you repay are stolen. The gold you give is returned to you—in blood.”

Poliziano’s face went white. “Lorenzo, in the name of mercy—”

“Mercy?” Lorenzo laughed. It was a cold, bitter sound. “Mercy is what they gave my father when they exiled him. Mercy is what they gave your family when they burned your books, Contessina. Mercy is what Savonarola preaches from his pulpit—and look how well that’s worked.”

A guard stepped forward, halberd raised. “My lord, this is treason.”

Lorenzo looked at him. “No. This is justice.”

The guard hesitated.

Then Lorenzo turned to the gathered servants and artisans. “The Medici ledgers are false. The Bruges discrepancy is real. The Strozzi Revenans account is true. And the man you trusted—”*he gestured to himself*—*is the one who built the bank you’ve been bleeding for two decades.”

Silence.

Then a woman in a wool dress stepped forward. “He’s mad.”

“Or honest,” said a young scribe.

Poliziano’s voice cracked. “Lorenzo—stop this. Before it’s too late.”

Lorenzo ignored him. He jumped down from the fountain and walked toward the palace gates.

Contessina followed, her dagger still at her belt.

Poliziano caught up, breath ragged. “You’re going to the Mercato Nuovo? To the guild hall? You’ll be arrested before you reach the door.”

Lorenzo didn’t stop. “Then arrest me.”

Contessina reached for his arm. He shook her off.

“You’re not doing this for Florence,” she said. “You’re doing it for revenge.”

Lorenzo turned. His eyes burned. “Is there a difference?”

A gust of wind tore through the courtyard, scattering petals from a Judas tree. The petals settled like blood on the stones.

Poliziano’s voice was a plea. “There is to me.”

Lorenzo looked at him. Really looked.

Then he smiled.

Not the smile of a banker. Not the smile of a friend.

The smile of a man who has nothing left to lose.

“Then pray for me, Agnolo. Because tonight, Florence will learn the true face of Medici justice.”

He turned and walked through the gates.

Contessina and Poliziano stood in the courtyard, surrounded by whispers, petals, and ruin.

Poliziano unfolded the ledger page again. His hands were shaking.

Contessina watched Lorenzo disappear into the alley.

Then she turned to Poliziano.

“He’s going to burn the city down.”

Poliziano didn’t answer.

He already knew.

Chapter 13

The pulpit smelled of damp wood and candle wax. Contessina Pazzi adjusted her black veil, the fabric coarse against her fingers. Below her, the nave of San Marco hummed with restless energy—nearly a hundred souls packed into a space meant for half that, their murmurs rising like steam from a cauldron.

Fra Savonarola stood at the base of the podium, his Dominican robes the color of dried blood. He did not look up. His hands were clasped, his head bowed as if in prayer. But his fingers tapped restlessly against the wood.

Contessina descended the spiral stairs, each step echoing in the hollow chamber. The friar’s eyes flicked to her as she emerged beside the altar. “You’re late,” he murmured.

“I know.”

He exhaled through his nose, then turned to the crowd. The murmurs died. “Blessed are the meek,” he began, “for they shall inherit the earth.”

The congregation knelt. Contessina did not.

Savonarola’s voice dropped, low and urgent. “But woe to those who hoard wealth in gilded coffers while their neighbors starve. Woe to those who call themselves shepherds yet feed on the flock.”

A ripple passed through the crowd. A few heads turned toward the back of the church.

Contessina kept her eyes on Savonarola’s sandals.

“Last week,” Savonarola said, “the Medici Bank called in a debt from the wool guild. Sixty-five households now sleep in the streets. Children beg where once they learned their letters. This is the face of Medici justice.”

A woman in the front row gasped. A man clutched his wife’s sleeve.

Contessina reached into her sleeve and pulled out a sheet of vellum. She had copied the entries herself—every forged transfer, every siphoned coin, every fraudulent signature. She unfolded it with deliberate slowness.

Savonarola paused. His gaze settled on the page. His fingers twitched.

“This,” Contessina said, her voice steady, “is the ledger of the Medici Bank. And this—” she tapped a line near the bottom—“is the handwriting of Lorenzo de’ Medici himself.”

The silence that followed was not awe. It was the kind of silence that precedes a scream.

Savonarola recovered first. “You accuse the Magnificent of forgery?”

“I accuse him of theft,” Contessina said. “Of murder. Of ruin. Of twenty years of lies.”

A young novice near the front whimpered. An elderly man crossed himself.

Savonarola’s jaw tightened. “These are serious accusations, Contessina. Especially on the eve of the Pazzi anniversary. Do you wish to reignite the fire?”

“No,” she said. “I wish to douse it in truth.”

She held the page higher. The candlelight caught the ink, made the Medici lion on the watermark glow like a wound.

Then she spoke the words she had rehearsed a hundred times in her chamber.

“In the name of the Pazzi, in the name of the dead, I declare: the Medici Bank does not belong to Lorenzo or his sons. It belongs to Florence. And today, we take it back.”

The first stone struck the floor at her feet. Then another. A third. The congregation erupted—not in prayer, but in ruin. Pages tore. Candles were overturned. A bench splintered against the wall.

Savonarola did not move to stop them. His eyes burned with something like triumph. Or fear.

Contessina didn’t flinch. She watched the crowd, her veil pulled low, her grip tight on the last unbroken ledger page.

Then the doors at the back of the church burst open.

Dust swirled in the sudden light. A figure stood silhouetted—tall, cloaked, unmistakable.

Lorenzo de’ Medici had returned.

He stepped forward. No guards. No retinue. Just the man himself, his face gaunt, his eyes fever-bright.

The crowd stilled. Even Savonarola fell silent.

Lorenzo looked at Contessina. Not with anger. Not with pleading. With something worse.

Recognition.

He walked toward her, his boots scuffing against the broken tiles. The silence followed him like a shadow.

Contessina did not lower the page. She held it up, letting the wind from the open doors stir the vellum.

Lorenzo stopped two paces away. Close enough to read the entries. Close enough to see the tears in the paper where she had clenched it too hard.

“You,” he said. “You were supposed to be an enemy.”

Contessina didn’t answer.

Savonarola stepped forward. “Lorenzo de’ Medici, you are accused of fraud, theft, and treason against the Republic of Florence.”

Lorenzo didn’t look at him. His gaze stayed on Contessina. “And you,” he said softly. “You were supposed to wear a crown of jewels. Not carry a torch in the dark.”

Contessina’s fingers trembled. For the first time in years, she felt the weight of her name. Pazzi. Not bride. Not daughter. Not victim.

Warrior.

She lifted the page higher. “This is the face of Medici justice.”

Lorenzo’s lips parted. A breath escaped—almost a laugh. Then he exhaled, slow and measured, as if he had been holding his breath for twenty years.

He reached out.

Not to take the page. To touch her wrist.

His fingers were cold.

“You think this changes anything?” he asked, voice low so only she could hear.

She didn’t pull away. “It changes everything.”

“No,” he said. “It only changes who gets to burn the city first.”

His grip tightened. A second. A heartbeat.

Then he let go.

Lorenzo stepped back. He turned to Savonarola. “You preach fire, friar. But fire purifies only what it consumes.”

Savonarola’s face darkened. “And you, stripped of your throne, still believe you rule.”

Lorenzo smiled. It was a terrible thing to see. “I never believed in thrones. Only ledgers.”

He walked toward the back of the church, where the doors stood open to the Piazza San Marco. The night air rushed in, carrying the smell of rain and burning.

Contessina watched him go.

Then she folded the ledger page, tucked it into her sleeve, and turned to Savonarola.

“Burn the rest,” she said. “Burn every copy. Every rumor. Every lie.”

The friar’s eyes narrowed. “And if I refuse?”

Contessina pulled her dagger. The blade caught the light.

Not to threaten him.

To cut the last thread between them.

“I will burn Florence myself,” she said. “Then I will find Lorenzo de’ Medici. And we will finish what the Pazzi began.”

She sheathed the dagger.

Then she walked past him, through the stunned crowd, into the night.

Chapter 14

The Mercato Nuovo smelled of damp wool and roasting chestnuts. The triangular arch of the *Lanzi Loggia* cast long shadows across the cobblestones, where money changers still haggled despite the afternoon’s chill. Lorenzo Strozzi stepped beneath the shade, his boots clicking against the stone. He wore no cloak today—only a dark doublet, the Medici silver thread barely visible in the fading light.

Across the square, Contessina Pazzi stood beneath the Porcellino’s bronze snout, her black dress blending with the shadows. She did not smile when she saw him. No greeting. No reproach. Just the tilt of her chin, the way her gloved fingers tightened around the ledger she carried.

Lorenzo walked toward her. The noise of the market faded—bickering merchants, clinking coins, the occasional shout. He stopped two paces away.

“You signed a death warrant today,” he said.

Contessina didn’t flinch. “I signed a ledger.”

“You accused the Magnificent. You called for the bank’s ruin. That’s not a ledger. That’s an execution.”

She unrolled the parchment just enough for him to see the Medici lion’s watermark. “I signed my name to the truth. Not to your vengeance.”

Lorenzo looked at the names scrawled at the bottom—each a Medici creditor, each a victim of fraud. His name wasn’t there. Neither was Contessina’s. But the ink was fresh, the hand bold.

“You could have burned it,” he said.

“I could have. But fire doesn’t build. It only destroys.”

A money changer nearby dropped a stack of florins. They scattered across the stones. Lorenzo didn’t move to help. Neither did Contessina.

“Then what do you want?” he asked.

“Not what,” she said. “Who.”

She turned slightly. Poliziano stood at the edge of the loggia, his poet’s cap pulled low, his hands clasped behind his back. He did not approach. He only watched.

Lorenzo exhaled through his nose. “You brought a witness.”

“He already knows,” she said. “He knew before San Marco. Before the pulpit. Before you ever let me see the truth.”

Lorenzo’s jaw tightened. “And you trust him?”

Contessina rolled the ledger shut with a snap. “I trust that he won’t let you burn Florence to the ground.”

Poliziano stepped forward at last, his boots clicking against the stones. “Lorenzo,” he said, voice low. “You’re playing a game you can’t finish.”

“Finishing was never the point,” Lorenzo said.

“Then what is?”

Lorenzo looked at Contessina. The ledger was still in her hands. The parchment crinkled as she adjusted her grip.

“You have two choices,” he said. “Burn the ledgers. Let the Medici collapse. Watch the city starve while the strong feast. Or sign them over to me.”

Contessina’s eyebrows lifted. “Sign them over to you?”

“As trustee,” he said. “With Poliziano as witness. We take control of the Medici Bank—not to ruin it, but to rebuild. Every coin that was stolen, every debt falsified—we return it. Every guild that was crushed, every family that was ruined—we restore.”

She stared at him. “And in return?”

“You take your place beside me,” he said. “Not as a Pazzi. Not as my enemy. As my partner.”

Poliziano’s mouth twitched. “You’re asking her to marry you in the middle of a coup.”

Lorenzo didn’t look at him. “I’m asking her to help me undo what the Medici did. To Florence. To you. To me.”

Contessina’s fingers hovered over the ledger. “And what do *you* get?”

“Everything,” he said. “The bank. The ledgers. The power to reshape Florence in a week what took them twenty years to destroy.”

She laughed. Not a sound of joy. A sound of realization. “You don’t want revenge. You want to be Lorenzo de’ Medici.”

“I want to be the man who saved Florence from the men who ruined it,” he said.

Contessina stepped closer. The ledger brushed against her skirts. “And if I refuse?”

“Then you let the Medici burn. You let Savonarola’s fire consume the city. And you live with the blood on your hands when the Pazzi name dies with you.”

She was close enough now to see the flecks of gold in his eyes. Close enough to smell the ink on the ledger, the sweat on his collar, the faint scent of gunpowder from the training yard.

Poliziano shifted. “Lorenzo, stop—”

“No,” Lorenzo said. “She deserves the choice.”

Contessina stared at the ledger. The names were real. The fraud was real. The possibility was real.

She unclasped her reticule and pulled out a quill. The feather was worn, the shaft chewed at the tip.

Lorenzo didn’t reach for it. He didn’t rush her. He stood still, his hands loose at his sides.

She uncorked the inkwell with her teeth. The quill dipped. The nib touched the parchment.

A single drop of ink bloomed.

Then she wrote.

Her signature was small. Deliberate. Final.

She blotted it with sand, then rolled the ledger closed. The snap of the parchment was louder than the market, louder than the city, louder than the past twenty years.

She held it out to him.

Lorenzo took it. His fingers brushed hers. The contact was electric.

Poliziano’s breath hissed between his teeth.

Contessina stepped back. “You have your ledgers, Lorenzo Strozzi. Now show me what kind of Florence you’ll build.”

He didn’t answer. He only unrolled the parchment and read the names again—each one a promise, each one a threat.

Somewhere in the distance, a church bell tolled.

The Pazzi anniversary was three days away.

Chapter 15

The balcony overlooked the Piazza della Signoria, where the sun hung low and heavy, bleeding gold across the rooftops. Below, Florence pulsed—merchants shouting, guildsmen parading, women balancing baskets on their heads. The city still didn’t know it was being reshaped.

Lorenzo stood at the balustrade, the Medici ledgers warm in his hands. The parchment crackled as he unrolled them, the ink glinting in the afternoon light. He hadn’t worn the black doublet today. Instead, a crimson surcoat hung from his shoulders, embroidered with silver thread that caught the sun like scattered coins. The people below would assume it was Medici regalia. They wouldn’t recognize the blood in the stitching.

Fra Savonarola stood in the crowd, his dark robe blending into the shadows of the Loggia dei Lanzi. He didn’t preach. Didn’t gesture. Just nodded once, as if he’d been expecting this moment for years.

Contessina Pazzi arrived without announcement. The crowd parted as she ascended the stairs, her black dress swallowing what little light remained. Lorenzo turned as the door creaked open, his pulse steady. He’d imagined this meeting in silence, in the dark, in the moments when guilt threatened to unravel him. But now she was here, and the ledgers in his hands felt heavier than the Medici name.

She stopped three steps below him. “You called for me.”

“I called for Florence,” he said.

“Which means the same thing.”

A gust of wind lifted the ledgers, flipping a page. Lorenzo clamped them shut. The sound made Contessina flinch. Or maybe it was something else.

“You signed the ledgers,” he said. “You made me your partner. Not just in name, but in truth.”

“And now you stand as if you own them.”

“I do own them.”

She stepped up beside him, close enough for him to smell the lavender in her hair, the faint salt of the Arno on her skin. “You want to be the Magnificent. You want to wear his crown without the blood on your hands.”

Lorenzo didn’t answer. Below, Poliziano emerged from the crowd, his poet’s cap tilted against the sun. He didn’t climb the stairs. He only watched, hands clasped behind his back, a spectator to the unraveling.

Contessina leaned forward, resting her forearms on the balustrade. “Look at them. They think you’re him. They think you’ve become Lorenzo de’ Medici.”

“And what do you think?”

She turned her head slightly, her profile sharp against the distance. “I think you’re the man who burned his father’s name to light the way.”

The ledgers trembled in his grip. A single page fluttered loose, drifting toward the stones below. Lorenzo didn’t move to catch it. The wind carried it higher, then lower, as if deciding where to land.

Contessina reached out. Her fingers brushed the parchment just as it settled on the balustrade. She didn’t pick it up. She only stared at the ink, the cramped handwriting, the date scrawled in the corner—*Bruges, 1472*.

“This is the first page,” she said.

“It’s the first sin.”

She picked it up. The parchment was brittle, the edges frayed. She turned it over. The other side bore a single line, written in cipher—*Strozzi Account: 2,000 florins. Transfer to Naples. Approved by L.*

Contessina’s breath caught. She looked at Lorenzo. “You kept this.”

“I built everything on this.”

She held the page up, letting the light catch the ink. “You framed it.”

The ledgers were gold-trimmed now. Framed like relics. Like absolution.

“A confession,” she said. “For them. Not for you.”

Lorenzo set the ledgers down on the balustrade. “I don’t need forgiveness.”

“Then why show me this?”

“Because you asked for the truth.”

She crumpled the page in her fist. The action was impulsive, violent. Poliziano took a step forward, as if to intervene.

Contessina didn’t release the parchment. She crushed it tighter, her knuckles white. “You could have burned it. You could have made them believe you were him. But you left a trail. You left *signs*.”

Lorenzo watched the crowd below, the way the light fractured through the glass of the Palazzo’s windows. “I wanted them to see.”

“See what? That you’re no better than he was?”

“No. That I’m the man who undid him.”

She looked away, her throat working. The wind tugged at her veil, pulling it loose from its pins. She didn’t fix it.

Poliziano cleared his throat. “Lorenzo. The people—”

“Are watching,” Lorenzo finished.

Below, the murmur of the crowd stilled. Heads turned upward. Someone pointed. A gasp rippled through the square.

Lorenzo followed their gaze.

On the balustrade, nestled between the ledgers, lay the crumpled page. But it wasn’t crumpled anymore. The gold frame around it glinted in the sun, catching the light like a jewel. The cipher was still visible, the ink darker where she’d crushed it, but now the parchment was encased in gilt.

The crowd below began to cheer.

Poliziano’s mouth fell open. “You—”

Lorenzo didn’t let him finish. He picked up the framed page, holding it high where everyone could see the confession, the sin, the proof that the Medici had been undone by their own ledger.

Contessina didn’t stop him. She only watched, her face pale, her grip on the balustrade tight enough to blanch her knuckles.

The people below roared. Some dropped to their knees. Others wept. A child pointed at the balcony, shouting something Lorenzo couldn’t hear.

He lowered the frame. The gilt caught the light one last time before it fell silent in his hands.

Contessina turned to him, her voice barely rising above the noise. “You didn’t do this for Florence.”

“No,” he said. “I did it for me.”

She looked at the framed page, then back at him. “Then you’re no better than they were.”

The ledgers sat between them, gold-trimmed and gleaming. The people below believed they were holding wisdom. The truth was written in the ink, in the gilt, in the silence between them.

Poliziano stepped forward. “Lorenzo. You can’t—”

Lorenzo dropped the frame. It struck the stones below, shattering the glass. The page fluttered free, landing in the dirt where the city’s feet would trample it.

The crowd’s cheer died.

Silence fell.

Contessina didn’t look down. She looked at Lorenzo, her eyes dark, her lips parted as if she might say something. But no words came.

He knew what she was thinking. That the ledgers were real. The fraud was real. The power was real.

And so was the cost.
